name: Princeton University Store
desc: The Princeton University Store is a not-for-profit cooperative society whose members share in the profits. As a co-op, it operates differently than an ordinary corporation. It is governed by a Board of Trustees made up of students, faculty, administrators, and alumni.

name: Forbes
desc:
In 1927 the main building was constructed as a hotel and became the Princeton Inn. The inn operated until 1970 when the University acquired the property and converted it into a college-like residence to accommodate a growing student population as women were admitted.

Known as the Princeton Inn College, the University constructed a dining hall and connected the main building to the former hotel's working quarters. In those days the inn housed 375 undergraduate students.

In 1984 Malcolm Forbes, Class of 1941, donated $3 million dollars to convert the inn into a Residential College. His generosity allowed for renovations to the dining hall and common areas, and when the construction was completed, the Princeton Inn College was renamed Forbes College in honor of Malcolm’s son, Malcolm “Steve” Forbes, Class of 1970.

name: Bowen
desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. At the center is the Engineering Quadrangle, or EQuad, a complex of connected wings with a courtyard, 25 classrooms, 120 laboratories, a machine shop, more than 125 faculty offices and study spaces, and a cafe. The engineering honor society, Tau Beta Pi, offers tours of the EQuad, and a self-guided tour handout is available outside room C-205. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.\n

name: Sherrerd
desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. At the center is the Engineering Quadrangle, or EQuad, a complex of connected wings with a courtyard, 25 classrooms, 120 laboratories, a machine shop, more than 125 faculty offices and study spaces, and a cafe. The engineering honor society, Tau Beta Pi, offers tours of the EQuad, and a self-guided tour handout is available outside room C-205. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.

name: E Baker
desc: First occupied in 2007.
Donated by the Class of 1981. First occupied in 2007.

name: Bloomberg
desc: Named for Emma Bloomberg, Class of 2001. First occupied in 2004.

name: Jadwin Gym
desc: Jadwin Gymnasium, with its five levels and enough floor space for eight football fields, is home to Princeton men's and women's teams for basketball, indoor track and field, squash, and fencing. It also includes a large indoor turf field, indoor tennis courts, main-court seating for nearly 7,000, and a game floor named Carril Court after Hall-of-Fame basketball coach Pete Carril. From its perch near Lake Carnegie, Jadwin is near other athletic facilities such as DeNunzio Pool, Princeton Stadium, and Weaver Track.
Donated by the Class of 1967. First occupied in 2009.

name: E
desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. At the center is the Engineering Quadrangle, or EQuad, a complex of connected wings with a courtyard, 25 classrooms, 120 laboratories, a machine shop, more than 125 faculty offices and study spaces, and a cafe. The engineering honor society, Tau Beta Pi, offers tours of the EQuad, and a self-guided tour handout is available outside room C-205. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.

desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. The engineering honor society, Tau Beta Pi, offers tours of the EQuad, and a self-guided tour handout is available outside room C-205. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.

name: Bogle
desc: Donated by John Bogle, Class of 1951. First occupied in 2009.

name: Yoseloff
desc: Donated by Anthony Yoseloff, Class of 1996. First occupied in 2009.

name: Frick
desc: Natural Sciences Neighborhood: Many of Princeton's mathematics and science departments are clustered along Washington Road in the vicinity of Lewis Library and Icahn Laboratory, allowing students and faculty to collaborate on interdisciplinary research. In addition, many of the structures feature distinctive historical or architectural details. Guyot Hall, which houses the geosciences department, features stone carvings of 200 plants and animals. Jadwin Hall, home of the physics department, displays models and writings from Benjamin Franklin and Joseph Henry, a leading American scientist and University professor from 1832 to 1846. Designed by architect Minoru Yamasaki, Peyton Hall holds the astrophysical sciences department and hosts monthly open houses to observe the night sky with one of its telescopes, with graduate students available to answer questions. The new, state-of-the-art Chemistry Building will include numerous energy-saving technologies, and the new Streicker Bridge spanning Washington Road will help strengthen the connections among the science community.
Donated by the Class of 1976. First occupied in 2009.

name: Robertson Hall
desc: Robertson Hall is the home of the Princeton School of Public and International Affairs, Princeton's renowned program for careers in public service. The building includes classrooms, offices, a lecture hall, and a small cafe, and hosts many lectures open to the public. With its series of 58 tapered columns, Robertson Hall is one of Princeton's finest examples of Modernist architecture. Minoru Yamasaki, also architect of the University's Peyton Hall and the former World Trade Center in New York City, designed Robertson Hall. The adjacent Scudder Plaza features a reflecting pool and Fountain of Freedom, a sculpture by James Fitzgerald.

name: Wilf
desc: Wilf Hall was constructed in 2009 of warm red brick, accented with horizontal bands of limestone. It stands just behind Scully Hall and is connected to 1976 Hall. Six rooms on the fourth floor are home to Independent juniors and seniors, who share a kitchen. The remaining floors of Wilf Hall house residents of Butler College.

name: Icahn
desc: Completed by architect Rafael Vinoly in 2002 (who also designed Princeton Stadium), the Icahn Laboratory houses the Lewis-Sigler Institute for Integrative Genomics, an interdisciplinary research and teaching center. The building's two-story, light-filled atrium is designed for conversation and engagement, with a cafe, public seating, and a prominent conversation piece--a curved metal-and-wood sculpture by Frank Gehry that doubles as a conference room. From the exterior, the building's south facade features an arcade of 40-foot aluminum louvers designed to control the building's heating. Lattice-like bands of aluminum in the louvers cast a woven shadow on the floor that evokes the double-helix structure of DNA. The arcade also connects Icahn Lab to "the ellipse," an arc of buildings overlooking the Poe and Pardee athletic fields. Natural Sciences Neighborhood: Many of Princeton's mathematics and science departments are clustered along Washington Road in the vicinity of Lewis Library and Icahn Laboratory, allowing students and faculty to collaborate on interdisciplinary research. In addition, many of the structures feature distinctive historical or architectural details. Guyot Hall, which houses the geosciences department, features stone carvings of 200 plants and animals. Jadwin Hall, home of the physics department, displays models and writings from Benjamin Franklin and Joseph Henry, a leading American scientist and University professor from 1832 to 1846. Designed by architect Minoru Yamasaki, Peyton Hall holds the astrophysical sciences department and hosts monthly open houses to observe the night sky with one of its telescopes, with graduate students available to answer questions. The new, state-of-the-art Chemistry Building will include numerous energy-saving technologies, and the new Streicker Bridge spanning Washington Road will help strengthen the connections among the science community.

name: Scully
desc:
Scully Hall is located in the southeastern section of campus and, along with Bloomberg Hall and the Icahn Lab, forms an elliptical border just above Poe and Pardee intramural fields. Scully Hall was built in 1998 and funded largely by a donation from John Scully, Class of 1966.

The complex was built with three wings that form the sides of a central courtyard—a Princeton tradition. It also features casement windows and towers that are reminiscent of the Collegiate Gothic style found in much of the residential architecture on campus.

Each wing has its own kitchen, laundry and study rooms, and the south wing has a wide archway topped by a modern version of a Gothic tower.

name: Wu
desc:
Wu Hall, built in 1983, is the most recent in this string of landmark, direction-shifting structures. And like its predecessors, Wu is important on several levels: stylistic, cultural, institutional.

On the one hand, this Robert Venturi '47 design symbolizes Princeton's refound commitment to architectural excellence. Nothing of its caliber had been built since the University Chapel, in the late 1920s. At the same time, Wu Hall also reflects the University's efforts to address its chronic problems in providing adequate living, dining, and social facilities for undergraduates. And it illustrates the University's strategy for upgrading its physical plant well into the 21st century. As such, Wu Hall fully merits comparison with other icons of the Princeton campus.

The road that led to the creation of Wu Hall began during the late 1960s and early 1970s, when University planners began to consider seriously the shape of the campus in a coeducational future. With the enrollment increases of the early 1960s, the undergraduate student body had grown by 1,500 to about 4,000 and Princeton had been able to add only about 1,000 new dormitory spaces.

name: Jadwin
desc: Natural Sciences Neighborhood: Many of Princeton's mathematics and science departments are clustered along Washington Road in the vicinity of Lewis Library and Icahn Laboratory, allowing students and faculty to collaborate on interdisciplinary research. In addition, many of the structures feature distinctive historical or architectural details. Guyot Hall, which houses the geosciences department, features stone carvings of 200 plants and animals. Jadwin Hall, home of the physics department, displays models and writings from Benjamin Franklin and Joseph Henry, a leading American scientist and University professor from 1832 to 1846. Designed by architect Minoru Yamasaki, Peyton Hall holds the astrophysical sciences department and hosts monthly open houses to observe the night sky with one of its telescopes, with graduate students available to answer questions. The new, state-of-the-art Chemistry Building will include numerous energy-saving technologies, and the new Streicker Bridge spanning Washington Road will help strengthen the connections among the science community.

name: University Chapel
desc: In 1928, Princeton dedicated the University Chapel designed by Gothic Revival architect Ralph Adams Cram. Among its distinguishing features are stained glass windows and furnishings that honor Biblical figures, Christian epics, and prominent Princetonians such as James Madison, the fourth president of the United States and a 1771 alumnus. The building materials include Pennsylvania sandstone, Indiana limestone, wood from Sherwood Forest in England, and a mid-16th-century pulpit imported from France. Additionally, the grand Mander/Skinner Organ, installed in 1928, features 109 stops and 8,000 pipes, including 60 trompettes en chamade (pipes mounted horizontally outside the organ case). The University Chapel, which seats 2,000, now serves as a site for daily prayer and meditation, Sunday services, and campus and community celebrations. The building also serves as a boundary of McCosh Courtyard, which includes McCosh Hall, Dickinson Hall, and the Mather Sundial, a 20-foot replica of the historic Turnbull Sundial constructed in 1551 at Corpus Christi College, Oxford.

name: Walker
desc: Donated by the Walker family and named for James Theodore Walker, Class of 1927. First occupied in 1930.

name: Wright
desc: Patton-Wright Hall, originally called Patton Hall, stands along Elm Drive and is well-known for its large bay windows and stately tower. Monkey clown gargoyles dressed in pointed hats, ruffs, and pantaloons watch over Patton-Wright.\n\nPatton-Wright’s convenient location near Dillon Gymnasium and Frist Campus Center make it popular with seniors. The Classes of 1880 and 1892-1901 donated funds to build this dormitory, and in 1999 the northern section of the building was renovated with funds donated by Bagley Wright, Class of 1946, and renamed Wright Hall.

name: Moffett
desc: Moffett Laboratory, which adjoins the eastern wing of Guyot Hall, was built in 1960 to provide additional space for the Department of Biology. Its cost was defrayed jointly by the Whitehall Foundation and the National institutes of Health, and it was named for George M. Moffett '04, founder of the Whitehall Foundation and donor in 1952 of the Moffett Professorship of Biology.\n\nIn addition to teaching laboratories and offices, Moffett Laboratory contains radioisotope laboratories and many types of specialized equipment for the study of physiological and biochemical processes, greenhouse space and plant growth chambers for the study of plant development, and a variety of different temperature-controlled rooms for studies on a wide range of biological phenomena.

name: Chancellor Green
desc: Chancellor Green and East Pyne Hall: Built in the late 1800s, these two buildings by architect William Appleton Potter have served generations of Princeton students. The connecting structures made up the University Library until Firestone Library was built in 1948. Chancellor Green then served as a student center, while East Pyne Hall became an administrative center. Today, Chancellor Green contains a cafe and seminar rooms, as well as a beautiful octagonal rotunda featuring stained glass windows and a large dome. East Pyne houses many language and humanities department offices and classrooms, and includes a courtyard and many sculptural details on its brownstone exterior.

name: Feinberg
desc: Donated and named by David Harold Feinberg, Class of 1963, and Carol Feinberg. First occupied in 1986.

name: Palmer House
desc: Princeton University's charming, 19th century guesthouse offers patrons the finest in genuine hospitality, numerous guest services, amenities and personalized attention for our guests. Overnight accommodations at Palmer House are open to official guests of Princeton University exclusively.

name: Cuyler
desc: Cuyler Hall is located along Elm Drive, just across from Dillon Gymnasium, and houses juniors and seniors. The archway connecting Cuyler Hall to Patton Hall features a vaulted ceiling with an intricate pattern of bosses that form a beautiful bouquet of stone flowers.\n\nCuyler Hall was built in memory of long-time trustee Cornelius C. Cuyler and was dedicated in 1912 by his classmate Woodrow Wilson. The Classes of 1881, 1882, 1891, William Evans Guy, Class of 1865, and John Oliver Halstead Pitney, Class of 1881, donated the funds to build Cuyler Hall.

1903 Hall stands just to the east of Cuyler Hall and west of Frist Campus Center. It was completed in 1930 and, along with Walker and Joline Halls, was the last construction project undertaken for over a decade at Princeton University. Today 1903 Hall houses both juniors and seniors.

name: Brown
desc: Brown Hall, built in1892 and renovated in 2010, is located south of the art museum and across Elm Road from Dillon Gymnasium. Susan Dod Brown donated the funds to build it and named it in memory of her late husband, David B. Brown. Brown Hall houses juniors and seniors.

name: Class of 1887 Boathouse
desc: Considered one of the finest rowing facilities in the nation, the Shea Rowing Center is the home for Princeton rowing. It stands next to Lake Carnegie and the Albridge C. “Bud” Smith III ‘36 Memorial Race Course, where the Tigers hold their home races.

name: Peyton
desc: Natural Sciences Neighborhood: Many of Princeton's mathematics and science departments are clustered along Washington Road in the vicinity of Lewis Library and Icahn Laboratory, allowing students and faculty to collaborate on interdisciplinary research. In addition, many of the structures feature distinctive historical or architectural details. Guyot Hall, which houses the geosciences department, features stone carvings of 200 plants and animals. Jadwin Hall, home of the physics department, displays models and writings from Benjamin Franklin and Joseph Henry, a leading American scientist and University professor from 1832 to 1846. Designed by architect Minoru Yamasaki, Peyton Hall holds the astrophysical sciences department and hosts monthly open houses to observe the night sky with one of its telescopes, with graduate students available to answer questions. The new, state-of-the-art Chemistry Building will include numerous energy-saving technologies, and the new Streicker Bridge spanning Washington Road will help strengthen the connections among the science community.

name: Frist Campus Center
desc: The Frist Campus Center is a bustling meeting place and service center for students, faculty, staff, and visitors. In addition to serving as a central gathering place for academic and student organizations, the center is a popular social and dining spot. Visitors will also find a Welcome Desk, the Frist Ticket Office, copy machines, ATM machines, and television lounges. Built around a former physics laboratory, the renovated exterior is featured in the television show House M.D. Princeton iconography - from historical photographs to a fiberglass tiger shark model - is located throughout the building.

name: F
desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. At the center is the Engineering Quadrangle, or EQuad, a complex of connected wings with a courtyard, 25 classrooms, 120 laboratories, a machine shop, more than 125 faculty offices and study spaces, and a cafe. The engineering honor society, Tau Beta Pi, offers tours of the EQuad, and a self-guided tour handout is available outside room C-205. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.

desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.

name: Aaron Burr
desc: Aaron Burr Hall, named in 1977 for Princeton's second president, occupies the southeast corner of Washington Road and Nassau Street. Built in 1891 as a chemical laboratory, it later housed the Department of Chemical Engineering and is now the home of the Department of Anthropology.

name: J
desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. At the center is the Engineering Quadrangle, or EQuad, a complex of connected wings with a courtyard, 25 classrooms, 120 laboratories, a machine shop, more than 125 faculty offices and study spaces, and a cafe. The engineering honor society, Tau Beta Pi, offers tours of the EQuad, and a self-guided tour handout is available outside room C-205. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.

name: Computer Science
desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. The engineering honor society, Tau Beta Pi, offers tours of the EQuad, and a self-guided tour handout is available outside room C-205. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.\n

name: G
desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. At the center is the Engineering Quadrangle, or EQuad, a complex of connected wings with a courtyard, 25 classrooms, 120 laboratories, a machine shop, more than 125 faculty offices and study spaces, and a cafe. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. name: C
desc: Five buildings house the School of Engineering and Applied Science. At the center is the Engineering Quadrangle, or EQuad, a complex of connected wings with a courtyard, 25 classrooms, 120 laboratories, a machine shop, more than 125 faculty offices and study spaces, and a cafe. The engineering honor society, Tau Beta Pi, offers tours of the EQuad, and a self-guided tour handout is available outside room C-205. The EQuad's garden-like courtyard features several pieces of sculpture. The engineering school also includes Bowen Hall; the Computer Science Building, which connects to the Friend Center; and Sherrerd Hall.

name: Old Graduate College
desc: The Graduate College, a complex of residential and dining areas for Princeton's graduate students, is located at the western edge of campus, where part of the Revolutionary War Battle of Princeton was fought. Crafted by Gothic Revival architect Ralph Adams Cram in 1913 and later expanded, the compound provides a peaceful setting for reflection and study. Cram also designed the University Chapel. The college's distinguishing feature is Cleveland Tower, named after U.S. President Grover Cleveland, who served as a University trustee and spent his retirement in Princeton. The 173-foot tower is topped by four limestone pinnacles and features a carillon of 67 bells, used to perform a Sunday concert series throughout the summer.

name: Hargadon
desc: Named for Fred A. Hargadon, Princeton's Dean of Admission from 1988 to 2003. First occupied in 2007.

name: Dinky Bar & Kitchen
desc: Dinky Bar and Kitchen

name: Clio
desc: From the front facades, Whig and Clio halls look identical. The structures by architect A. Page Brown were built in 1893 in the Ionic style of a Greek temple, replacing earlier buildings made of wood and stucco. The buildings were named after and used by the Whig and Clio student debating societies, which merged in 1928. The American Whig-Cliosophic Society, the oldest college debating club in the United States, now resides in Whig Hall. Clio Hall is now the home for the Graduate School's administrative offices.

name: Murley-Pivirotto
desc: Named for University trustee Robert S. Murley, Class of 1972 alumnus, and his wife, Mary Pivirotto Murley, Class of 1976. First occupied in 2007

name: Whig
desc: From the front facades, Whig and Clio halls look identical. The structures by architect A. Page Brown were built in 1893 in the Ionic style of a Greek temple, replacing earlier buildings made of wood and stucco. The buildings were named after and used by the Whig and Clio student debating societies, which merged in 1928. The American Whig-Cliosophic Society, the oldest college debating club in the United States, now resides in Whig Hall. Clio Hall is now the home for the Graduate School's administrative offices.

name: Joline
desc: Donated by Mary Joline in honor of Adrian Joline, Class of 1870. First occupied in 1933.

name: Campbell
desc: Donated by the Class of 1877 and named for John A. Campbell, Class of 1877. First occupied in 1909.

name: Hamilton
desc: Donated by the Class of 1884 and 1885 and named for John Hamilton. First occupied in 1911.

name: Spelman
desc: Designed by renowned architect I. M. Pei, Spelman Halls is a series of eight dormitories with apartment-style suites. Built in the Modernist style, the triangular buildings and diagonal spaces feature ribbon windows, flat precast concrete walls, and metal tubing. The dormitories were named for Laura Spelman Rockefeller, who, with her husband, John D. Rockefeller Sr., helped form Spelman College in Atlanta, Georgia, the first American college for black women. The Pablo Picasso sculpture Head of a Woman looks over the lawn south of the complex.

name: Pyne
desc:
Pyne Hall was the first of the interwar dormitories constructed to meet the housing needs of a growing University population. Pyne Hall was built in 1922 and is less embellished than neighboring Henry and Foulke Halls, but in the spring magnolias along University Place bloom prolifically and grace its facade.

Pyne Hall stands just south of 1901 and Laughlin Halls and forms a U-shape facing north. Shortly after its construction, the University planted six elm trees in the Pyne Hall courtyard to honor the Princeton Naval Unit of World War I. Both juniors and seniors live in Pyne Hall.

The Classes of 1902, 1906, 1908, 1920, 1921, 1922, 1923 and alumni and friends donated the funds to build Pyne Hall.

name: Nassau Hall
desc: Today Nassau Hall is used for administrative offices, but this light brown sandstone structure named for England's King William III, of the House of Nassau, was designated a national landmark in 1960 for serving as a model for colonial college structures and as a barracks in the Revolutionary War. In the Battle of Princeton in 1777, British and American troops quartered there at different times and a cannonball that hit the south wall of the west wing left a scar that is visible today. Nassau Hall became the nation's capitol when the Continental Congress met there between June and November of 1783, and the Congress was there when it first learned that the British had signed a peace treaty granting independence to the former colonies. The bronze tigers on either side of the front steps were designed by sculptor A. P. Proctor and presented in 1911 by Woodrow Wilson's classmates, and from those steps some of the campus's oldest buildings are visible - Stanhope Hall, Maclean House, and Henry House. This site, known as the Front Campus, is the setting for Commencement ceremonies, from which new Princeton graduates symbolically leave campus through FitzRandolph Gate. Behind Nassau Hall is Cannon Green, a lawn under which a Revolutionary War cannon is partially buried. The green is bounded by landmark campus buildings including East Pyne Hall, West College, and the twin Whig and Clio halls.

name: Henry
desc: Henry Hall, built just after World War I, features graceful stone carvings and an ornate archway that connects Foulke Hall and leads to University Place. Henry Hall stands on the site of the former Pennsylvania Railroad station and was part of the “Western Wall” of the University in the 1920s. Friends and family of Howard H. Henry, Class of 1904, donated the funds to build Henry Hall.

name: Dillon Gym
desc: Built in 1947, Dillon Gym is the campus' intramural sports and physical education headquarters with four basketball courts, 19 squash courts, an extensive fitness center and a pool.

name: 1901-Laughlin
desc: 1901-Laughlin Hall was built in 1925 and is home to mostly juniors, although seniors may live there too. It stands just west of Pyne Drive and, along with Foulke and Henry Halls, forms a long quadrangle looking north to Blair Arch. In the 2002 film “A Beautiful Mind,” John Nash makes his first appearance at Princeton University in this quad.

name: Foulke
desc: Foulke Hall is one of several dormitories built as the student body grew just after World War I. Foulke and neighboring Henry Hall were erected on the site of the former Pennsylvania Railroad station just southwest of Blair Hall and formed the “Western Wall” of the University in the 1920s. The building was financed with donations made in honor of classmates lost in the war.

name: Lauritzen
desc: Gift of the Lauritzen family. First occupied in 2007.

name: Lockhart
desc: Lockhart Hall was completed in 1928 and was the last of the dormitories built along the “Western Wall” of the University in the early part of the twentieth century. It stands north of Foulke Hall and just south of the U-store along University Place. Lockhart features an archway that is slightly off center and frames McCosh Walk. James H. Lockhart, Class of 1887, donated funds to construct the building.

name: Dod
desc: Dod Hall was built along Elm Road, just southeast of Edwards Hall, to accommodate a student population that had grown significantly under the presidency of James McCosh in the late 1800s.  As with Brown Hall, Susan Dod Brown donated the funds for construction but named it for her brother, Princeton math teacher Albert Baldwin Dod. Dod Hall houses juniors and seniors.

name: Little
desc: Little Hall was built in 1901, parallel to the railroad tracks that ended at the Pennsylvania Station just below the Blair Arch. Today it stands at the end of Pyne Drive and is shared by Mathey College and upperclassmen. Little Hall was the first dormitory at Princeton to have indoor bathrooms, and with several additions, the building was later connected to the gymnasium. Little’s most notable feature is a four-story tower with a large bay window and the University coat of arms supported by a pair of tigers.

name: Edwards
desc: Named for the third president of the University Jonathan Edwards. First occupied in 1880.

name: Witherspoon
desc: Donated by the Trustees of the College of New Jersey and named for John Witherspoon. First occupied in 1877.

name: Blair
desc: Blair Arch: One of the most beloved spots on campus is Blair Arch, at the juncture between Blair and Buyers halls. Built in 1897, Blair Hall marked the western edge of campus for 20 years and its architects, the firm Cope &amp; Stewardson, were masters of the Collegiate Gothic style. The arch also served as a gateway to the University for passengers disembarking from the train into town. Due to its exceptional acoustics, Blair Arch now is the setting for performances by more than a dozen student a cappella groups; it is also the site of traditional step sings by the freshman class at the beginning of the academic year and by the senior class before Commencement.

name: Holder
desc: Named for Margaret Olivia Sage and Christopher Holder. First occupied in 1910 and renovated in 2005.

name: Fisher
desc: Donated by members of the Fisher family (Classes 1976, 1979, 1983). First occupied in 2007.

name: Princeton Stadium
desc: Built in 1997, Princeton Stadium is a facility simplistic in goals and complex in structure. Princeton Stadium was built with more than just football Saturdays in mind; it stands as an extension of the campus, integrated into the daily life of the University. The stadium concourses are always open, and the facility has served as a meeting and banquet place for many constituents.  Princeton Stadium seats 27,800 with room for up to 30,000.

name: Roots Ocean Prime
desc: Fancy restaurant offering a variety of seafood including crab, fish & lobster, plus steaks & wine.

name: Art on Hulfish
desc: Art on Hulfish, the Art Museum’s new gallery at 11 Hulfish Street in downtown Princeton, will showcase a roster of exhibitions led by photography that consider questions and themes of significance to 21st-century life. In addition to presenting four exhibitions each year through early 2025, Art on Hulfish will host a rich schedule of related programming—including University discussion group meetings, hands-on learning opportunities, and drop-in activities.

name: Construction in the Third and Fourth Dimension
alt: Construction in the Thirdand Fourth Dimension

name: Lewis Library
desc: This building of curved stainless steel, sloping glass, stucco, and orange brick stands out even among Princeton's eclectic architectural landscape. Completed by Frank Gehry in 2008, the 87,000-square-foot Lewis Library houses the University's combined science libraries as well as a technology wing. Decorated in bright blues, yellows, oranges, and greens, as well as Gehry's signature light wood tones, the building is a popular study space for students. One of the most stunning features is the 'treehouse,' reading room on the second floor that skims the tree line. The library accepts tour requests through its website, http://scilib.princeton.edu. Natural Sciences Neighborhood: Many of Princeton's mathematics and science departments are clustered along Washington Road in the vicinity of Lewis Library and Icahn Laboratory, allowing students and faculty to collaborate on interdisciplinary research. In addition, many of the structures feature distinctive historical or architectural details. Guyot Hall, which houses the geosciences department, features stone carvings of 200 plants and animals. Jadwin Hall, home of the physics department, displays models and writings from Benjamin Franklin and Joseph Henry, a leading American scientist and University professor from 1832 to 1846. Designed by architect Minoru Yamasaki, Peyton Hall holds the astrophysical sciences department and hosts monthly open houses to observe the night sky with one of its telescopes, with graduate students available to answer questions. The new, state-of-the-art Chemistry Building will include numerous energy-saving technologies, and the new Streicker Bridge spanning Washington Road will help strengthen the connections among the science community.

name: Firestone Library
desc: Built in 1948 and expanded several times, the Harvey S. Firestone Memorial Library is the main campus library. As one of the most distinguished research libraries in the world, the University library system contains more than 13 million holdings in 11 buildings. Firestone is the repository for many of the humanities and social science collections and the Department of Rare Books and Special Collections. Additionally, it houses several areas open to the public. The Cotsen Children's Library offers programming and a whimsical gallery, and Firestone's Main Gallery and Milberg Gallery feature items from the library's rich collections.

name: Prospect House
desc: Since 1968, Prospect House has served as a dining and reception facility for faculty and staff, overlooking a lush, five-acre garden. This national historic landmark has an engaging background--the original stone farmhouse hosted several sessions of the Continental Congress in 1783; the farmhouse was replaced in 1852 with the current Italianate villa designed by John Notman; and in 1878 the property was deeded to the University for use as the president's residence. Woodrow Wilson was among the six Princeton presidents who lived there and his wife Ellen designed the garden's current form, which echoes the Princeton shield. The garden includes a colorful array of 8,000 flowers against a background of evergreens.

name: Princeton University Press
desc: Founded in 1905, Princeton University Press is a nonprofit publisher with close connections to Princeton University. The Press brings influential voices and ideas to the world stage through their academic scholarship, advancing the frontiers of scholarly knowledge and promoting the human conversation.

Princeton’s only vegetarian co-op, but you may join even if you are not a vegetarian. As a co-op member you will cook dinner once a week with other members (no experience necessary!) and are welcome to eat at 2 Dickinson every night of the week. You may use the co-op kitchen any time of the day or night, or enjoy leftovers, fruit, cereal, bread and anything else from a well-stocked pantry.

2 Dickinson cooks are inventive, so you never eat the same meal twice. Recently members have made home-made tortilla chips, spanikopita, Thai peanut noodles, teriyaki tofu, vegan macaroni and cheese, chocolate chip cookies and baklava.

2 Dickinson has an extensive collection of cookbooks and orders a diverse array of produce and dry goods each week, so that all members can find something to suit their preferences.

name: Carl A. Fields Center
alt: Carl A. Fields Center for Equality and Cultural Understanding

name: Bobst
alt: Bobst Center for Peace and Justice

name: Princeton University Art Museum
desc: The new Princeton University Art Museum is a bold reimagining of a campus and community treasure. The museum features nine interconnected pavilions, 32 galleries, and a 12,000-square-foot education center, all centered around the luminous Grand Hall. With expanded gallery space, a third-floor restaurant, and welcoming art walks that invite visitors to linger, study, or simply enjoy the view, PUAM is more than a museum, it’s a vibrant cultural hub.

name: Wallace Dance & Theater
desc: The Wallace Theater is a 3,600 square foot black box theater designed to support a variety of production types including drama, musicals, dance, and multimedia events. The multi-form theater can seat up to 150 in various flexible audience configurations and includes a mezzanine level seating gallery. The adjacent Theater Annex functions as an extension to the black box theater stage or can be walled off as a separate space. The theater, finished in perforated black metal and equipped with overhead catwalks for lighting and rigging, features the newest technology in LED lighting for theatrical production and offers video and projection capabilities. The theater is also equipped with portable speakers for customizable audio playback, as well as a 6’ Boston Baby Grand Piano.

name: Richardson Auditorium in Alexander Hall
desc: Completed in 1894, Alexander Hall is home to Richardson Auditorium, a premier convocation and performance venue on campus. Alexander Hall was designed by architect William Appleton Potter, who also designed the University's Chancellor Green and East Pyne Hall, and collaborated on Witherspoon Hall. The first public lecture in the building was given in November 1894 by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle, author of the Sherlock Holmes mystery novels. The 891-seat concert hall is renowned for its acoustical quality, and the structure is admired for its architectural detail. The Romanesque building features an exterior of granite and brown sandstone, and a wealth of academically themed stained glass, mosaics, sculptures, and carvings, both inside and out.

name: Princeton Garden Theatre
desc: The Garden Theatre is Princeton’s original cinema, built in 1920. Today, the Garden is an independent, nonprofit cultural institution dedicated to presenting the finest indie, international, documentary, and classic films, series, and educational programs. The Garden preserves the classic theater experience at the historic, walkable theater combined with premiere presentation and sound. The Garden fosters entertainment, exploration, and community engagement through diverse and thoughtful programming.

name: Bedford Field
desc: Completed in the summer of 2012, Bedford Field is a state-of-the-art facility dedicated solely to Princeton field hockey. The multimillion-dollar stadium is adjacent to Class of 1952 Stadium, where the Princeton men's and women's lacrosse teams play.

name: Sherrerd Field
desc: Sherrerd Field at Class of 1952 Stadium is the home of Princeton's men's and women's lacrosse programs.

desc: TigerTransit operates frequently during weekdays until early evening.

desc: Open to faculty and staff only until 5 PM. Permit required. Open to visitors 5 pm - 2 am weekdays and 6 am - 2 am Saturdays and Sundays. Recommended for events starting after 5 pm at the University Chapel, Prospect House and Murray-Dodge Theatre.

name: University NOW Day Nursery
desc: 185 Broadmead

name: C.U.B.
desc: The CUB (central utility building) is a single-story structure with geo-exchange facilities to deliver thermal energy for buildings at the new Meadows Neighborhood. In addition to the main building, there will be two Thermal Energy Storage (TES) Tanks, electrical transformers, and a natural-gas powered back-up generator that are visually unified with the main building with architectural screening and landscape.

name: Class of 1986 Fitness and Wellness Center
alt: Class of 1986 Fitness and Wellness Center

name: Weaver Track Stadium
desc: Weaver Stadium, Princeton's state-of-the-art stadium for outdoor track and field, provides available seating for nearly 2,500 spectators and includes a videoboard at one end of the track. Outside of the warmup/jogging lane, the oval has an additional eight lanes across, with each lane 1.22 meters in width and features an impressively long radius of 36.5 meters on the turns. There are dual runways and competition areas for all field events including the long jump, triple jump, pole vault, shot put, discus and javelin.

name: Mamoun's Falafel
desc: Iconic Middle Eastern falafel and shawarma spot. Princeton student staple since the 1970s.

name: Tacoria
desc: Fast-casual Mexican restaurant with burritos, tacos, and bowls. Popular late-night spot.

name: Alfalfa
desc: Health-conscious restaurant with salads, grain bowls, and smoothies.

name: Lan Ramen
desc: Authentic Japanese ramen house with rich broths and handmade noodles.

name: Hoagie Haven
desc: Legendary Princeton sub shop. The 'Phat Lady' and 'Sanchez' are student favorites. Open since 1974.

name: Bent Spoon
desc: Artisanal ice cream and coffee. Locally sourced, seasonal flavors. Princeton institution.

name: Thomas Sweet
desc: Ice cream, chocolates, and sweet treats. Known for 'blend-ins'. Princeton classic.

name: Olives
desc: Mediterranean and Middle Eastern cuisine with falafel, hummus, and kebabs.

name: Agricola Eatery
desc: Farm-to-table restaurant and market with locally sourced seasonal dishes.

name: Triumph Brewing Company
desc: Craft brewery and gastropub with house-brewed beers and American fare.

name: Nassau Inn
desc: Historic Princeton hotel with the Yankee Doodle Tap Room. Landmark since 1756.

name: Jammin Crepes
desc: Sweet and savory crepes made fresh to order. Popular brunch spot.

name: Kung Fu Tea
desc: Bubble tea and fruit tea chain. Popular with Princeton students.

name: Small World Coffee
desc: Independent coffee shop. Local favorite for studying and hanging out. Two locations.

name: Rojo's Roastery
desc: Specialty coffee roaster with single-origin beans and espresso drinks.

name: J.Crew
desc: Classic American clothing retailer.

name: Labyrinth Books
desc: Independent academic bookstore serving Princeton University. Course books, literature, and gifts.

name: Princeton Public Library
desc: Public library serving the Princeton community. Free Wi-Fi and study spaces.

name: Princeton Family YMCA
desc: Community fitness center with pool, gym, and classes.

name: Mehek
desc: Indian cuisine with tandoori, curry, and biryani dishes.

name: Teresa's
desc: Italian-American restaurant and bar. Casual dining with pizza and pasta.

name: Winberie's
desc: American grill and bar in Palmer Square. Popular for brunch.

name: Hunan Garden
desc: Chinese restaurant with Szechuan and Hunan specialties. Student favorite for delivery.

name: Mistral
desc: French-Mediterranean cuisine. Princeton fine dining.

name: Ivy League Barber Shop
desc: Classic Princeton barber shop. Popular with students and locals.

name: Princeton Record Exchange
desc: Legendary independent record store with new and used vinyl, CDs, and DVDs. Princeton institution since 1980.

name: CVS Pharmacy
desc: Pharmacy and convenience store.

name: Wawa
desc: Convenience store with fresh hoagies, coffee, and snacks. 24/7.
